items.title: A/B test: new prompt vs baseline
items.scenario: An ML team runs 500 queries through a baseline prompt and 500 through a revised prompt, measuring task success rate. Baseline: 72%; new: 76%. They test H₀: p_new − p_baseline = 0 vs H₁: difference ≠ 0 at α = 0.05.
items.explanation: Even if the new prompt is genuinely better, sampling variation could produce a 4-point gap by chance. The test quantifies whether 76% vs 72% is extreme enough to reject "no improvement."

items.title: Mean latency after optimization
items.scenario: After a caching change, 40 API calls show mean latency 118 ms (s = 22 ms). Historical baseline μ₀ = 130 ms. One-sample t-test: H₀: μ = 130 vs H₁: μ < 130 (one-sided).
items.explanation: t = (118 − 130) / (22/√40) ≈ −3.45. A negative t in the right direction supports "latency decreased"; the p-value asks how often such a large drop occurs if μ were still 130.

items.title: Clinical-style before/after on the same users
items.scenario: Ten users complete a task with Model A then Model B (paired design). Differences d_i = score_B − score_A have mean d̄ = 0.8, s_d = 1.2. Paired t-test on H₀: mean difference = 0.
items.explanation: Pairing removes user-to-user noise; the test uses d̄ and s_d/√10 instead of treating the two samples as independent.

items.title: Benchmark claim: "statistically significant" lift
items.scenario: A blog post reports F1 improved from 0.81 to 0.83 on 200 test examples and calls it "significant" without stating H₀, α, or test. A skeptical reader asks whether 0.02 could arise from label noise alone.
items.explanation: Without a defined test and sample-size context, "significant" is ambiguous. Hypothesis testing forces explicit defaults and error rates before interpreting small metric shifts.
